story:
The ground drops out from under Liora before she can scream. She lands in a narrow shaft of stone slick with moss, a circle of sky far above. Her voice echoes when she calls for Kai, then thins into silence. Water chills her ankles; the walls offer nothing. She has known darkness all her life, but this is different—no passage, no direction, just waiting, as unreachable as the sky.

She tries to hold onto the memory of the surface: wind against her skin, sunlight warm on closed eyes. It has only been weeks since she came aboveground after a lifetime in tunnels, yet already the memory slips. Now the thought of being buried again tightens her chest. She calls for Kai until her voice goes hoarse. The echo comes back wrong, as if the dark has swallowed it.

A new fear roots in her: what if ghosts can’t reach this deep? What if he can’t hear her? What if she dies in this forgotten place? She presses her forehead to the cold stone to prove she is still here—flesh and breath—though grief and panic press hard against her ribs.

A dull shimmer in the water draws her eye. She reaches into the chill and lifts an old dagger. Tarnished, heavy, wrong in her hand, it still glints faintly. Etched into its golden hilt is a symbol she knows: the same spiral-and-edge motif that coils around Kai’s wrist. He said it was old, older than him. Now it is here, humming subtly against her skin. The well seems to shift with that hum—whispers rising from the water, or only her breath in a new shape.

She whispers Kai’s name. The stone throws it back, thinner, unfamiliar. For a heartbeat she thinks she hears him answer, but there is only the drip and her breath. The truth seeps in cold: Kai isn’t coming down. Whether he can’t or won’t, the result is the same. She is alone.

The circle of sky feels farther. Liora sets her palm to the rough wall, testing for holds. If no one can save her, she will climb. She considers leaving the dagger, then presses it to her chest and begins.

Her first attempt fails. The wall is slick; her boots skid; her satchel drags at her shoulder. She slips and hits the water hard, the shock stealing her breath. She sprawls in the cold shallows, lifts a hand, and traces the circle of sky in the air with her fingertips, refusing the thought of never feeling daylight again. Not an option.

She draws the dagger and uses it as a wedge, driving the tip between stones, letting its faint vibration steady her. The hum deepens with each pull—up, up, up. By the time the blade snags and her footing goes out, instinct takes over. She drags the edge along the wall to slow the fall until it catches. Pain sparks through her arm, but she sets her boot, pushes, and climbs again. The world can try to bury her. It will not keep her.

Her fingers find the rim. She hauls herself onto damp grass and lies there, gulping the cool evening air. Then a voice: “About time. I was starting to think you’d redecorate down there.” Kai stands nearby, arms crossed, ghostlight faint around him, like he’s been waiting all along. She laughs, shaky. He could have helped. He grins—why ruin a dramatic escape?

The dagger still hums beside her. She does not yet know what its mark means, or why it found her, but she is alive, and the sky is wide.
